- Tight pairing of CLI and core layers with no intermediary utilities keeps the fix feature easy to trace from command entry to side-effect — a positive sign of focused, single-responsibility design.
- Both unit tests reside in the same zone as the source, which is correct for a small feature slice; ensure integration-level fix scenarios (e.g. multi-item repair) are covered in the broader integration suite.
- prd-fix-operations has exactly 1 cross-zone import in each direction with prd-cli-operations and no coupling to any other zone — the minimal footprint is a model for how small, focused feature slices should integrate with the broader architecture.
- The fix zone's minimal cross-zone footprint (1 import each direction with prd-cli-operations only) gives it the cleanest dependency profile among the source zones and demonstrates the value of keeping small features as isolated satellites rather than merging them into the nearest large zone.
- Zone name 'prd-fix-operations' uses a verb ('fix') as its domain identifier while all other prd-prefixed zones use noun phrases ('domain-operations', 'cli-operations', 'schema-foundation'). The verb naming makes it ambiguous whether 'fix' is a user-facing command name or a domain concept name — future zones for 'remove', 'merge', or 'prune' commands would face the same naming ambiguity.
- Rename 'prd-fix-operations' to 'prd-repair-operations' or merge it into 'prd-cli-operations' (its only cross-zone partner). The verb-based name is inconsistent with the noun-phrase convention of sibling zones and provides no disambiguation if additional single-command zones are added later.
